material.mattext: In ggplot2, which function initializes a plot object?
What does the + operator do in a ggplot2 statement?
Adds a new layer or component to the plot
In the ggplot2 template, aes() is short for:
A geometric object that represents data visually (e.g., point, line, bar)
Fuel economy data for American-made cars only
Fuel economy data for a variety of car models
Which variable in mpg records engine displacement in litres?
Which variable in mpg records highway miles per gallon?
What does mapping an aesthetic mean in ggplot2?
Linking a visual property (like color or size) to a variable in the data
Setting a visual property to a fixed value for all observations
Creating a legend entry for the plot
Filtering which rows of data to display
What does setting an aesthetic mean in ggplot2?
Linking a visual property to a variable in the data
Assigning a visual property a fixed constant value, outside of aes()
Specifying which aesthetic maps to x vs. y

What color will the points be when this code runs?

ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = “blue”))
Blue, because “blue” is a recognized color name
A reddish-orange color, because “blue” is treated as a data value and mapped to the default discrete color scale
What is the correct way to make all points in a geom_point() layer the color blue?
Which aesthetic controls the transparency of a geom?
When you map a continuous variable to the color aesthetic, ggplot2 produces:
A discrete set of colors, one per unique value
An error, because color requires a categorical variable
When you map a categorical variable to the color aesthetic, ggplot2 produces:
A discrete set of distinct colors, one per category
All points in different shades of gray
An error, because color requires a numeric variable
Where you place aes() — inside ggplot() versus inside a specific geom_*() call — determines:
Whether the mapping applies globally to all layers or only to that specific geom
Whether the legend is shown or hidden
Whether the aesthetic is mapped or set
What does geom_smooth() add to a plot by default?
A straight line from the minimum to the maximum data point
A smoothed curve (loess by default) with a confidence interval band
A horizontal line at the mean of y
How do you add a linear regression line (instead of the default loess smoother) with geom_smooth()?
How do you remove the shaded confidence interval band from geom_smooth()?
You map color = drv inside ggplot(aes(...)) and then add both geom_point() and geom_smooth(). What happens?
Only geom_point() uses the color mapping; geom_smooth() ignores it
Both layers inherit the color mapping and plot separately by drv
An error occurs because you can’t use a global color mapping with multiple geoms
geom_smooth() draws one overall line while geom_point() shows separate colors
You want separate smooth lines by drv, but points should NOT be colored by drv. Which code achieves this?
ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy, color = drv)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth()
Creates a separate subplot panel for each level of class
Filters the data to show only one class at a time
Wraps long axis labels for the class variable
What is the main practical difference between facet_wrap() and facet_grid()?
facet_wrap() is for categorical variables; facet_grid() is for continuous variables
facet_wrap() wraps panels from one variable into a grid; facet_grid() arranges panels in a strict row × column layout defined by two variables
facet_grid() is an older, deprecated version of facet_wrap()
In the formula facet_grid(drv ~ cyl), which variable defines the rows of the grid?
A single row of panels, one per level of cyl
A single column of panels, one per level of cyl
A full grid using all combinations of variables
An error; . is not valid in a facet formula
A single row of panels, one per level of drv
A single column of panels, one per level of drv
An error; . is not valid after the ~
By default, all panels in facet_wrap() share the same axis scales. Which argument allows each panel to have its own y-axis scale?
R4DS recommends using facets instead of color/shape aesthetics primarily when:
The categorical variable has many levels, making a single plot with overlapping colors hard to read
Which geom is most appropriate for visualizing the distribution of a single categorical variable?
Which geom is most appropriate for visualizing the distribution of a single continuous variable?
The width of each bin — how much of the x range each bar covers
The total number of bars to display
The default binwidth is always wrong and must be changed before submitting work
Different binwidths can reveal different patterns; no single default is best for every dataset
The binwidth must match the measurement unit of the variable
Canvas requires a specific binwidth for automated grading
Which geom shows the distribution of a continuous variable split across levels of a categorical variable?
What does the middle line in a geom_boxplot() represent?
The midpoint between the minimum and maximum
One standard deviation below the mean to one above
Two standard deviations below the mean to two above
In a geom_boxplot(), individual points plotted beyond the whiskers represent:
Data entry errors that should be removed before analysis
Values more than one standard deviation from the mean
Potential outliers — values beyond 1.5 × the IQR from the box edges
The top and bottom 5% of the distribution
Which function adds a title, axis labels, and a caption to a ggplot?
Which argument to labs() sets the plot title?
Which function saves a ggplot to a file?
The first plot created in the current session
A plot you specify by passing it as the first argument
All plots created since the last ggsave() call

What does this code produce?

ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
An error, because you cannot use two geoms with identical mappings
A scatterplot with a smooth line overlaid, both using displ and hwy
A more concise, idiomatic way to write the code in the previous question is:
Which of the following correctly maps cty to x and hwy to y using shorthand ggplot2 syntax (omitting argument names)?
Which statement about how ggplot2 handles missing values (NA) in mapped variables is correct?
ggplot2 replaces NAs with the variable mean before plotting
ggplot2 treats NA as a separate category in discrete scales
ggplot2 removes rows with NAs in mapped variables and shows a warning message
ggplot2 stops with an error if any mapped variable contains NAs
What is the purpose of the group aesthetic in ggplot2?
It assigns each group a distinct color
It tells ggplot2 to draw separate lines or curves per group without changing color or shape
It filters the data to display one group at a time
It combines multiple variables into a single composite grouping variable

To get a separate smooth line per drv group without coloring the line by drv, what should replace geom_smooth() in this code?

ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) + geom_smooth()
The layered grammar of graphics describes a plot as a combination of:
A dataset, a plot type, and a color palette
An x-axis, a y-axis, and a geom
Data, aesthetic mappings, and one or more geometric layers — plus optional scales, coordinates, and facets
A title, axis labels, and data points
Too many variables are mapped to aesthetics at once
Many data points share the same location, making it hard to see the density of observations
The smooth line obscures the underlying data points

What does this code produce?

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) + geom_bar()
A scatter plot of class values along the x-axis
A bar chart showing the count of cars in each vehicle class
An error, because geom_bar() requires both x and y aesthetics
In geom_bar(), if you want the bar heights to represent a value already in your data (not a count), you should use:
When troubleshooting a ggplot error, R4DS recommends first:
Deleting the plot and restarting from scratch
Carefully re-reading the code — checking for missing closing parentheses, misspelled names, and making sure + appears at the end of a line rather than the beginning of the next
Running fix(ggplot) to let R auto-correct the error
